title: Risk Assessment
controlText: Periodically assess the risk to organizational operations (including mission, functions, image, or reputation), organizational assets, and individuals, resulting from the operation of organizational systems and the associated processing, storage, or transmission of CUI.
plainEnglish: Conduct formal, documented risk assessments on a regular schedule and whenever significant changes occur. A risk assessment evaluates what could go wrong (threats and vulnerabilities), how likely it is, and what the impact would be to the organization and CUI. The output is a risk assessment report that informs security decisions, resource prioritization, and the selection and tailoring of controls. This is not a vulnerability scan — it is a broader analysis that considers threat sources, threat events, likelihood, and impact. Many organizations conflate risk assessment with vulnerability scanning; assessors distinguish between them. The Assessment Guide notes that risk assessments must be 'periodically' performed and must address operations, assets, and individuals.

objectives.text: the frequency to assess risk to organizational operations, organizational assets, and individuals is defined; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: A documented policy or procedure that defines how often risk assessments must be conducted — typically annually or after significant system changes. The frequency must be written down, not assumed. Look for the risk assessment policy, system security plan, or procedure that states the assessment cadence explicitly.
commonEvidence: Risk Assessment Policy defining assessment frequency
System Security Plan section specifying risk assessment schedule
Organizational procedures addressing risk assessment frequency

objectives.text: risk to organizational operations, organizational assets, and individuals resulting from the operation of an organizational system that processes, stores, or transmits CUI is assessed with the defined frequency.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Evidence that a formal risk assessment was actually performed on the defined schedule. The assessment must address risk to operations, assets, and individuals — not just technical vulnerabilities. Look for a risk assessment report dated within the required period, covering the in-scope system that processes, stores, or transmits CUI.
commonEvidence: Risk Assessment Report (current and previous cycle) with dates
Risk register or risk tracking documentation tied to the assessment
Management review and sign-off records for the risk assessment
System Security Plan section documenting risk assessment results

commonArtifacts: Risk Assessment Report (current and prior cycles)
System Security Plan section on risk assessment
commonGaps: Risk assessment was completed at system authorization five years ago and never repeated
Vulnerability scan results presented as the risk assessment — not the same thing
Risk assessment conducted but results never reviewed by leadership or acted upon
No defined frequency for risk assessments — 'we do it when we need to'
Risk assessment scope limited to IT assets — organizational operations, mission, and CUI transmission not addressed
Risk assessment report produced by a consultant and filed away without internal dissemination or follow-through
Risk register exists but is disconnected from the formal risk assessment process

title: Vulnerability Scan
controlText: Scan for vulnerabilities in organizational systems and applications periodically and when new vulnerabilities affecting those systems are identified.
plainEnglish: Run automated vulnerability scans against all in-scope systems on a regular schedule and whenever new significant vulnerabilities are disclosed. Scans must be authenticated (credentialed) to produce meaningful results — unauthenticated scans miss the majority of software vulnerabilities. Scan coverage must include the entire in-scope environment: workstations, servers, network devices, cloud resources, and applications. The output — a vulnerability scan report showing discovered vulnerabilities with severity ratings — must be retained and acted on. This is a -5 Derived practice, indicating that without functional vulnerability scanning the overall CMMC posture degrades significantly.

objectives.text: the frequency to scan for vulnerabilities in organizational systems and applications is defined;
objectives.whatToLookFor: A documented policy or procedure that specifies how often vulnerability scans must be performed on organizational systems and applications. The frequency must be written, not assumed. Look for the vulnerability management policy, patch management procedure, or system security plan section that states the scan cadence explicitly.
commonEvidence: Vulnerability management policy with defined scan frequency
Patch management procedure specifying scan schedule
System Security Plan section on vulnerability scanning frequency

objectives.text: vulnerability scans are performed on organizational systems with the defined frequency;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Scheduled vulnerability scans running against all in-scope organizational systems on the cadence defined in policy — typically monthly or quarterly at minimum. Scan tool configured with authenticated (credentialed) access. Scan reports dated within the required period. Coverage must include servers, workstations, network devices, and cloud infrastructure.
commonEvidence: Vulnerability scan reports from the most recent scan cycle(s) with dates
Scan schedule or scan job configuration showing frequency
Authenticated scan credential configuration
Asset list used as scan target inventory

objectives.text: vulnerability scans are performed on applications with the defined frequency;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Vulnerability scans covering applications — web applications, custom-developed software, and commercial applications — on the defined schedule. Application scans may use different tooling than infrastructure scans (e.g., DAST, web application scanners). Evidence that applications are included in scope, not just infrastructure.
commonEvidence: Application vulnerability scan reports with dates
Web application scanner configuration and scan results
DAST or application security scan records
Asset inventory showing applications in scan scope

objectives.text: vulnerability scans are performed on organizational systems when new vulnerabilities are identified; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Evidence that out-of-cycle scans are triggered on organizational systems when significant new vulnerabilities are disclosed — patch Tuesday scans, emergency scans after major CVEs (Log4Shell, ProxyLogon, etc.), or a defined process for evaluating and responding to new disclosures. Documented procedure describing triggers for unscheduled scans.
commonEvidence: Vulnerability scan reports showing out-of-cycle or ad-hoc scans
Documented vulnerability management procedure describing triggers for unscheduled scans
Records of targeted scans after major CVE disclosures
Vulnerability notification subscription records (NVD, CISA KEV, vendor alerts)

objectives.text: vulnerability scans are performed on applications when new vulnerabilities are identified.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Evidence that applications are re-scanned when new vulnerabilities affecting those applications are identified — not just organizational systems. Look for application-specific scan records triggered by CVE advisories, vendor security bulletins, or threat intelligence notifications relevant to the application stack.
commonEvidence: Application vulnerability scan reports triggered by new CVE disclosures
Documented procedure for application-specific scanning when new vulnerabilities are identified
Vendor security bulletin review records with linked application scan results
Change management tickets referencing application scans triggered by new vulnerabilities

commonArtifacts: Scan tool configuration showing authenticated scan setup
Patch deployment records tied to vulnerability findings
CISA KEV or NVD subscription records
commonGaps: Unauthenticated scans only — misses the majority of software vulnerabilities on local systems
Scans run against a subset of assets — cloud resources, network devices, or workstations excluded from scope
Scans run but results not analyzed or acted upon — 'scan and forget'
No process for out-of-cycle scans after major CVE disclosures
Vulnerability findings not tied to a remediation workflow — scan output is a report that sits in a folder
No defined SLA for remediation — critical vulnerabilities open for 6+ months
Scan credentials have insufficient privileges — scans authenticated but not credentialed with admin rights, producing incomplete results
Web application scanning not included — only infrastructure scans performed

title: Vulnerability Remediation
controlText: Remediate vulnerabilities in accordance with risk assessments.
plainEnglish: Discovering vulnerabilities is only valuable if you fix them. This practice requires a documented, risk-informed remediation process: prioritize based on severity and organizational risk context, remediate within defined timeframes, and verify that remediation was effective. The risk-informed framing matters — RA.L2-3.11.3 explicitly requires that remediation decisions align with the broader risk assessment from RA.L2-3.11.1. Organizations that patch everything with equal priority (or nothing at a critical severity) are not meeting this practice. Compensating controls and risk acceptance decisions must be documented when full remediation is not immediately possible.

objectives.whatToLookFor: Evidence that vulnerabilities have been identified in organizational systems and applications — typically through the scanning process in RA.L2-3.11.2. A current list of known vulnerabilities, vulnerability scan reports showing identified findings, or a vulnerability register. This objective confirms identification occurred, not just that scanning is scheduled.
commonEvidence: Vulnerability scan reports listing identified vulnerabilities
Vulnerability register or tracking list showing current open findings
Penetration test findings identifying vulnerabilities
Security assessment report with vulnerability findings

objectives.text: vulnerabilities are remediated in accordance with risk assessments.
objectives.whatToLookFor: A documented, risk-informed remediation process: prioritization based on severity and organizational risk context, remediation within defined timeframes, and verification that remediation was effective. Evidence that critical and high vulnerabilities are addressed faster than lower-severity findings. Remediation decisions (patch, mitigate, accept) traceable to documented risk rationale.
commonEvidence: Vulnerability remediation SLA or priority matrix tied to severity ratings
Remediation tracking records showing vulnerability status and completion dates
Patch deployment records showing remediation of identified vulnerabilities
Risk acceptance records for vulnerabilities accepted rather than remediated
Rescan results confirming successful remediation

commonArtifacts: Vulnerability management policy with remediation SLAs by severity
Remediation tracking records (vulnerability management platform or ticketing system)
Risk acceptance documentation for unremediable findings
Aging report showing open vulnerabilities by severity and time-open
commonGaps: No defined remediation timeframes — critical findings sit open indefinitely
Patches deployed on an irregular schedule not tied to vulnerability severity
Risk acceptance decisions made informally without documentation
Remediation tracked in the scan tool but not verified via rescan
Long-open critical vulnerabilities with no documented compensating controls or risk acceptance
Vulnerability remediation prioritized by ease rather than risk — easy wins patched, hard ones ignored
Remediation process exists but does not reference the organizational risk assessment — disconnected from RA.L2-3.11.1
